rule_statement: Classify a token as a noun or a verb before applying suffix or tense rules.
meaning_effect: Prevents noun possession rules from being confused with verb subject or object rules.

rule_statement: A noun ending in ?/?? can mark your (one man's) object.
meaning_effect: Adds second-person masculine singular possession to a noun.
exception_notes: Only apply after confirming the token is functioning as a noun.

rule_statement: A noun ending in ?? can mark your (one man's) plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds second-person masculine singular possession to a plural noun target.

rule_statement: Noun endings ?? and ??? mark your (many men's) singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds second-person masculine plural possession to a noun.

rule_statement: Noun endings ?/?? and ?? can mark his singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds third-person masculine singular possession to a noun.
exception_notes: The vav can be mistaken for a conjunction if the parser ignores word role.

rule_statement: A noun ending in ? can mark my singular object, and related variants can mark my plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds first-person singular possession to a noun.
exception_notes: Plural-looking forms can still be singular lexical exceptions elsewhere in the curriculum.

rule_statement: Noun endings ?? and ??? mark our singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds first-person plural possession to a noun.
exception_notes: Do not confuse noun possession ?? with verb subject or object suffix uses.

rule_statement: Noun endings ?/?? and ?? can mark her singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds third-person feminine singular possession to a noun.

rule_statement: Noun endings in the feminine ??-family mark your (one lady's) singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds second-person feminine singular possession to a noun.
exception_notes: Unpointed forms can be confused with the masculine singular possessive family.

rule_statement: Noun endings ? and ???/??? mark their (many men or mixed group) singular or plural objects.
meaning_effect: Adds third-person masculine or mixed plural possession to a noun.
exception_notes: The short ? ending can be a compressed form of a fuller plural-possessive family.

rule_statement: A verb ending in ?? marks you (one man) did in the past.
meaning_effect: Adds second-person masculine singular subject to a past-tense verb.
exception_notes: Only high-confidence after the token is classified as a verb.

rule_statement: A prefixed ? on a verb usually marks he will.
meaning_effect: Adds third-person masculine singular future subject marking to a verb.
exception_notes: Yod-initial roots may require nikud-sensitive handling.

rule_statement: A prefixed ?? before a future-form verb can preserve the future reading rather than forcing a past reading.
meaning_effect: Adds conjunctional and while keeping the future reading in the curriculum's introductory vav rule family.
exception_notes: This rule family must be separated from vav-hahipuch forms with different vocalization.

rule_statement: A prefixed ?? on many future-shaped verbs flips the reading to past narrative sequence.
meaning_effect: Turns many future-shaped verbs into past narrative forms such as and he did.
exception_notes: Guttural-initial verbs can shift the vocalization pattern.

rule_statement: A verb with prefixed ? and suffixed ?? can mean they (many men) will.
meaning_effect: Adds third-person masculine plural future subject marking to a verb.

rule_statement: A verb with prefixed ? can mean you (one man) will or she will, and context resolves the correct reading.
meaning_effect: Marks a future form whose subject reading is ambiguous without context.
exception_notes: This is an intentional ambiguity lane, not a one-answer rule.

rule_statement: A prefixed ? can mark I will and a prefixed ? can mark we will in the future system.
meaning_effect: Adds first-person future subject marking to a verb.
exception_notes: The prefixed ? can overlap with passive-like patterns elsewhere in the curriculum.

rule_statement: A regular bare verb form can mean he did in the past.
meaning_effect: Provides the default past 3ms reading for a regular unsuffixed verb form.
exception_notes: This is a real parsing rule, but bare citation-like forms are often low-value shoresh questions.

rule_statement: A verb ending in ?? can mean they did or a direct command to many men, depending on context.
meaning_effect: Creates an ambiguity between past plural and imperative plural readings.
exception_notes: Do not force a single reading without context.

rule_statement: Direct command forms can be recognized by pattern and by audience-marking endings such as ??, ?, and ??.
meaning_effect: Marks direct commands to masculine singular, masculine plural, feminine singular, or feminine plural audiences.
exception_notes: Some plural command forms overlap with past plural forms and must be disambiguated.

rule_statement: Present or habitual forms are taught with distinct patterning, including masculine singular, masculine plural, feminine singular, and feminine plural variants.
meaning_effect: Marks present or habitual meaning rather than simple past or future.
exception_notes: These forms should not be collapsed into the past/future system.

rule_statement: Verbs can take attached object pronouns such as me, you, her, them, or us.
meaning_effect: Adds a direct-object pronoun to a verb form without changing the underlying lexical verb.
exception_notes: Several exact subfamilies in the source are OCR-degraded, so the seed treats this as a high-confidence family rule only.

rule_statement: Some prefixed or vowel-shifted verb patterns indicate passive-like meaning or caused action rather than a simple active reading.
meaning_effect: Signals passive-like or causative-like meaning in a limited, curriculum-supported way.
exception_notes: Seeded broadly because some exact subrules are OCR-degraded.

rule_statement: Some forms that look plural on the surface do not behave like ordinary plural nouns for beginner parsing purposes.
meaning_effect: Blocks naive plural parsing for a small exception list taught explicitly in the curriculum.
exception_notes: These are exception-bank items, not productive rules.

rule_statement: When a form has more than one plausible parse, phrase or pasuk context determines the correct reading.
meaning_effect: Prevents the engine from forcing a single answer when morphology alone is insufficient.
exception_notes: This is a governing instructional rule, not a morphology trigger.
